Scenario.title: The CostaRicto Campaign: Complex Attack against Energy Sector
Scenario.overview: Energy Complex The CostaRicto campaign employs sophisticated techniques to gain unauthorized access to energy sector systems, maintain persistence, escalate privileges, and ultimately cause significant impact through ICS tampering.
Scenario.attack_surface: AVAILABLE_SURFACES: Internet-facing assets, Shared DB or directory, Vulnerability, Injection flaws, Custom APIs, SSL Certificates and Attribution, WHOIS records, contacts and history, Public and private cloud, Shadow IT, Poor implementation of access controls, Authentication issues
Scenario.attack_vector: AVAILABLE_VECTORS: Web, Account takeover, Application compromise, Vulnerability exploit, Malware, Man in The Middle(MiTM), Session hijacking, Brute-force attack, Distributed Denial of Service(DDoS), Watering-hole, Compromised and weak credentials, Phishing, Impersonations/Spoofing, Improper usage, Advanced Persistent Threat(APT)

stage.name: Reconnaissance
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 538 - Name: Open-Source Library Manipulation
stage.description: The CostaRicto campaign begins by gathering information about potential targets in the energy sector through open-source intelligence.

stage.name: Resource Development
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 517 - Name: Documentation Alteration to Circumvent Dial-down
stage.description: The attackers develop custom tools using readily available resources to aid in their intrusion efforts.

stage.name: Initial Access
stage.kev: CVE-2023-23752 [CWE-807]: Microsoft Exchange Server Security Feature Bypass
stage.description: The CostaRicto campaign gains initial access to target systems by sending spear-phishing emails with malicious attachments exploiting a vulnerability in Microsoft Exchange Server.

stage.name: Execution
stage.kev: CVE-2021-21985 [CWE-94]: Windows PowerShell Remoting Client Elevation of Privilege Vulnerability
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 579 - Name: Replace Winlogon Helper DLL
stage.description: Upon successful exploitation, the attackers use PowerShell to execute commands and establish a foothold within the compromised network.

Scenario.summary: The CostaRicto campaign demonstrates a complex, multi-stage attack against the energy sector, leveraging various tactics and techniques to achieve unauthorized access, maintain persistence, and ultimately cause significant impact through ICS tampering.
